description: Samples the closest source pixel without smoothing or blending. Best for pixel art, masks, icons, UI captures, and hard-edged artwork where every original pixel should remain crisp.
Blends the four nearest source pixels to create a simple smooth result. It is fast and predictable, but softer and less detailed than higher-quality filters.
Uses cubic interpolation over neighboring pixels for a balanced, polished resize. Good general-purpose quality for photos, smooth artwork, gradients, and most everyday image-size changes.
A smoother bicubic variant intended for upscaling images. It reduces jagged edges and stair-stepping when enlarging, at the cost of some sharpness and fine texture.
A sharper bicubic variant intended for reducing image size. It preserves apparent detail and edge definition while shrinking, though high-contrast artwork can become slightly crisp.
Averages the covered source pixels for each output pixel, especially when reducing size. Strong choice for shrinking images cleanly with fewer aliasing artifacts and less shimmer.
Uses a windowed sinc filter for high-quality resizing with strong detail retention. Often crisp and detailed, but may create subtle ringing or halos near hard edges.
Balanced cubic filter that aims to avoid both excessive blur and excessive ringing. It is a careful middle ground for natural-looking resizing without dramatic sharpening.
Sharper cubic filter that preserves detail and local contrast well. It can overshoot around high-contrast edges, so it may look crisp but slightly aggressive.
Smooth interpolation filter that is simple, stable, and useful for modest resizing. It favors gentle transitions over sharp detail and usually avoids strong artifacts.
Blurs with a Gaussian kernel for very smooth results and strong anti-aliasing when shrinking. Useful for reducing harsh edges, but it sacrifices sharpness and fine detail.
Cubic B-spline filter that produces very smooth output and removes visible aliasing. It is gentle and stable, but trades away fine detail and crisp edges.
Modern filter that combines smooth resampling with a controlled sharpening pass. It aims for crisp, lively results without obvious ringing artifacts or crunchy over-sharpening.
High-quality spline filter with wide support and strong reconstruction quality. It preserves detail well across both enlargement and reduction, but can be slower to compute.
Simplest averaging filter that gives each covered source pixel equal weight. Very fast and decent for simple reductions, especially halving sizes, but not very refined.
Tent-shaped linear filter, effectively equivalent to bilinear interpolation. Fast and smooth with minimal artifacts for small scale changes, but limited detail preservation.
Cubic filter tuned to minimize blur while avoiding visible halos. A balanced general-purpose option for photo resizing when you want natural detail without hard sharpening.
Sharper Robidoux variant that retains more detail and local contrast. Well suited to downscaling where crispness matters, but slightly more prone to edge emphasis.
Windowed sinc filter using a Blackman window for high-quality reconstruction. It usually rings less than Lanczos near hard edges while keeping much of the same detail.
Edge-preserving pixel-art upscaler that smooths jagged diagonals without general blurring. Ideal for retro sprites at integer scales when nearest-neighbor feels too blocky.
Advanced pattern-based pixel-art scaler that detects and interpolates edges. Produces smooth, clean upscales of low-resolution artwork while preserving the graphic character.
Higher-quality xBR variant that produces cleaner, less blocky upscales for sprites and low-resolution images. It focuses on smooth contours while avoiding ordinary blur.
Lookup-based pixel-art upscaler that smooths edges and curves for retro graphics. Designed for fixed 2x, 3x, or 4x scales where stylized enlargement is preferred.
Keeps pixels crisp by favoring hard edges and integer-like scaling behavior. Best for retro or low-resolution artwork where softness would destroy the intended pixel structure.
